title: The honest renovation
summary: An architect plans to convert a 1930s villa into modern flats, keeping only the facade. A historian argues that the facade is the cheapest part — and proposes a different kind of preservation.

body.speaker: An
body.text: Bác Vinh, before you say anything — I have not approved the demolition. The plan I have proposed is renovation with retained facade. Half the city would call that conservative.

body.speaker: Bác Vinh
body.text: Half the city has lost its history by exactly that kind of compromise. Tell me what you intend inside.

body.speaker: An
body.text: The wiring is from 1957. Some of the staircase risers are eight inches; people fall on them. The room layouts assume servants in the back, owners in the front. We will rewire, level the stairs, and re-plan the layout to be three flats.

body.speaker: Bác Vinh
body.text: And in the process you will destroy the very thing that makes the building a historical object. A 1930s villa is not the front wall. The front wall is the cheapest part of it. What makes it 1930s is the room sequence, the wood, the ratio of the windows to the floor, the smell.

body.speaker: An
body.text: The smell is a curtain of mildew. Nobody is buying mildew.

body.speaker: Bác Vinh
body.text: Granted. But the proportions are not mildew. The proportions are decisions. The architect in 1934 chose them on purpose. When you re-plan the layout, you erase those decisions. The facade left standing is a face with no body. People walking past will not know.

body.speaker: An
body.text: So your alternative is a museum. Whose budget?

body.speaker: Bác Vinh
body.text: I am not asking for a museum, An. I am asking for two things. First, that the major interior decisions — the staircase, the central hall, the ratio of ceiling to floor in the principal rooms — be kept where possible. Second, that the new work be visibly new. Do not pretend a new staircase is from 1934. Use modern materials honestly. Sign your date on it.

body.speaker: An
body.text: An honest renovation rather than a forged restoration.

body.speaker: Bác Vinh
body.text: Yes. A building should have visible layers, like a tree has rings. In a hundred years, someone walking through should be able to see 1934, and 2027, and know which is which. That is how a building keeps being historical. If you erase 2027 to look more 1934, you lie about both.

body.speaker: An
body.text: I can defend half of what you've said to my clients. The other half costs more than they will pay.

body.speaker: Bác Vinh
body.text: Then you have a real negotiation, not an architectural one. But please, before signing — invite me to walk the building one afternoon. There may be rooms whose proportions can be kept at almost no extra cost, and which would carry the building's character even if everything else changes.

body.speaker: Bác Vinh
body.text: Bring your tape measure. I'll bring my notes from 1981, when I was last inside.

annotations.meaningVi: sự thỏa hiệp, sự nhân nhượng
tỷ lệ, kích thước tương đối giữa các phần

grammarNotes.title: Zero conditional for moral or logical consequence
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 0 (if + thì hiện tại đơn, thì hiện tại đơn) thường dùng để diễn tả quy luật chung. Trong văn nghị luận, nó cũng được dùng để nói về hậu quả đạo đức hoặc logic mà người nói cho là chắc chắn: 'If you erase 2027 to look more 1934, you lie about both.' (Nếu anh xóa năm 2027 để trông giống năm 1934 hơn, anh đã nói dối về cả hai.) Đây không phải khả năng có thể — đó là một quan hệ nhân quả mà người nói coi là tất yếu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The zero conditional (if + present simple, present simple) usually states general truths. In argument, it is also used to declare a moral or logical consequence the speaker treats as inevitable: 'If you erase 2027 to look more 1934, you lie about both.' This is not a possibility — it is a relationship presented as certain.

Bác Vinh: Half the city has lost its history by exactly that kind of {{b2}}. Tell me what you intend inside.

Bác Vinh: And in the process you will {{b5}} the very thing that makes the building a historical object. A 1930s villa is not the front wall. The front wall is the cheapest part of it. What makes it 1930s is the room sequence, the wood, the {{b6}} of the windows to the floor, the smell.
An: The smell is a curtain of {{b7}}. Nobody is buying mildew.
Bác Vinh: Granted. But the {{b8}} are not mildew. The proportions are decisions. The architect in 1934 chose them on purpose. When you re-plan the layout, you erase those decisions. The {{b9}} left standing is a face with no body. People walking past will not know.
An: So your alternative is a {{b10}}. Whose budget?
Bác Vinh: I am not asking for a museum, An. I am asking for two things. First, that the major interior decisions — the staircase, the central hall, the ratio of ceiling to floor in the principal rooms — be kept where possible. Second, that the new work be {{b11}} new. Do not pretend a new staircase is from 1934. Use modern materials {{b12}}. Sign your date on it.
An: An honest renovation rather than a forged restoration.
Bác Vinh: Yes. A building should have visible layers, like a tree has {{b13}}. In a hundred years, someone walking through should be able to see 1934, and 2027, and know which is which. That is how a building keeps being historical. If you erase 2027 to look more 1934, you lie about both.
An: I can defend half of what you've said to my clients. The other half costs more than they will pay.
Bác Vinh: Then you have a real {{b14}}, not an architectural one. But please, before signing — invite me to walk the building one afternoon. There may be rooms whose proportions can be kept at almost no extra cost, and which would carry the building's character even if everything else changes.
An: I can do {{b15}}.
Bác Vinh: Bring your {{b16}} measure. I'll bring my notes from 1981, when I was last inside.

blanks.explanation: An's plan is renovation with retained facade — keeping the front wall while changing the inside.
Vinh: 'Half the city has lost its history by exactly that kind of compromise' — the facadism that keeps a wall and loses everything else.
The wiring is from 1957 — one of An's practical reasons to change the interior.
The room layouts assume servants in the back, owners in the front — a social structure An wants to remove.
'You will destroy the very thing that makes the building a historical object' — Vinh's strongest objection.
The ratio of the windows to the floor — one of the proportions Vinh says are deliberate decisions.
An's dismissal: 'The smell is a curtain of mildew. Nobody is buying mildew.'
Vinh: 'But the proportions are not mildew. The proportions are decisions.'
'The facade left standing is a face with no body.' Vinh's image of facadism.
An's challenge: 'So your alternative is a museum. Whose budget?'
Vinh's second condition: that the new work be visibly new — not disguised as period.
Use modern materials honestly — admit they are modern instead of faking the 1934 look.
A building should have visible layers, like a tree has rings — each year readable inside it.
Then you have a real negotiation, not an architectural one — the conflict moves from design to budget.
An offers Thursday for a walk-through.
Bring your tape measure — Vinh wants the meeting to be concrete, not abstract.

translationVi:
An: Bác Vinh, trước khi bác nói gì — cháu đã không duyệt việc phá dỡ. Phương án cháu đưa ra là cải tạo, giữ lại mặt tiền. Nửa thành phố sẽ gọi đó là bảo thủ.
Bác Vinh: Nửa thành phố đã đánh mất lịch sử của nó chính bằng kiểu thỏa hiệp đó. Cháu định làm gì bên trong, kể bác nghe.
An: Hệ thống điện từ năm 1957. Một số bậc cầu thang cao tới hai mươi phân; người ta ngã ở đó. Bố cục các phòng giả định có người hầu phía sau, chủ nhà phía trước. Bọn cháu sẽ làm lại điện, hạ cầu thang xuống độ cao chuẩn, và quy hoạch lại thành ba căn hộ.
Bác Vinh: Và trong quá trình đó, cháu sẽ phá hủy đúng cái thứ làm tòa nhà này là một di sản. Một biệt thự thập niên 1930 không phải là bức tường trước. Bức tường trước là phần rẻ nhất của nó. Cái làm cho nó là thập niên 1930 là chuỗi phòng, gỗ, tỷ lệ giữa cửa sổ và sàn, cả mùi.
An: Mùi là một bức màn nấm mốc. Không ai mua nấm mốc.
Bác Vinh: Công nhận. Nhưng các tỷ lệ thì không phải nấm mốc. Các tỷ lệ là những quyết định. Người kiến trúc sư năm 1934 đã chọn chúng có chủ đích. Khi cháu quy hoạch lại bố cục, cháu xóa đi những quyết định đó. Mặt tiền còn đứng nguyên là một gương mặt không có thân. Người đi ngang sẽ không biết.
An: Vậy phương án thay thế của bác là một bảo tàng. Ngân sách của ai?
Bác Vinh: Bác không xin một bảo tàng, An. Bác xin hai điều. Một, là các quyết định bên trong quan trọng nhất — cầu thang, gian giữa, tỷ lệ giữa trần và sàn ở các phòng chính — được giữ ở những chỗ có thể. Hai, là phần xây mới phải nhìn thấy được là mới. Đừng giả vờ một cầu thang mới là từ năm 1934. Hãy dùng vật liệu hiện đại một cách thành thật. Ghi năm của mình lên đó.
An: Một sự cải tạo trung thực, chứ không phải sự phục dựng giả mạo.
Bác Vinh: Đúng. Một tòa nhà nên có các lớp nhìn thấy được, như cây có vòng. Một trăm năm sau, người đi xuyên qua nó phải nhận ra được 1934, và 2027, và biết cái nào là cái nào. Đó là cách một tòa nhà tiếp tục là di sản. Nếu cháu xóa đi 2027 để trông giống 1934 hơn, cháu nói dối về cả hai.
An: Cháu có thể bảo vệ được nửa những gì bác nói trước chủ đầu tư. Nửa kia tốn kém hơn họ sẵn lòng trả.
Bác Vinh: Vậy là cháu có một cuộc thương lượng thực sự, không phải một cuộc tranh luận kiến trúc. Nhưng trước khi ký — mời bác đi xem tòa nhà một buổi chiều. Có thể có những phòng mà tỷ lệ của chúng có thể giữ được mà gần như không tốn thêm chi phí, và những phòng đó sẽ giữ lại tính cách của tòa nhà cả khi mọi thứ khác đổi.
An: Cháu rảnh thứ Năm.
Bác Vinh: Mang theo thước cuộn. Bác sẽ mang ghi chú của bác từ năm 1981, lần cuối bác vào trong.

questions.prompt: What is An's current proposal for the 1930s villa?
options: Renovation with the facade retained, converting the inside into three flats
questions.explanation: 'The plan I have proposed is renovation with retained facade.'

questions.prompt: Why does Bác Vinh disagree with this kind of compromise?
options: He says half the city has lost its history by exactly that kind of compromise — keeping a face, losing everything else
He wants the building demolished and rebuilt
questions.explanation: 'Half the city has lost its history by exactly that kind of compromise.'

questions.prompt: What three practical reasons does An give for changing the interior?
options: Wiring from 1957, tall staircase risers people fall on, and room layouts that assume servants in the back
questions.explanation: Each is named in An's third turn.
questions.hint: Look at An's turn about the wiring.

questions.prompt: According to Bác Vinh, what makes the building 'a 1930s villa'?
options: The room sequence, the wood, the ratio of the windows to the floor, and the smell
questions.explanation: 'What makes it 1930s is the room sequence, the wood, the ratio of the windows to the floor, the smell.'
questions.hint: Look at Vinh's first long response about the villa.

questions.prompt: What does Vinh call the front wall, by comparison?
questions.explanation: 'The front wall is the cheapest part of it.'
questions.hint: Same turn — Vinh's hierarchy of value.

questions.prompt: What image does Vinh use to describe a building whose facade is kept but whose interior is gutted?
questions.explanation: 'The facade left standing is a face with no body.'
questions.hint: Look at Vinh's turn that begins 'Granted.'

questions.prompt: What two things is Vinh actually asking for?
options: (1) That the major interior decisions — staircase, central hall, ceiling-floor ratios in principal rooms — be kept where possible; (2) that any new work be visibly new
A historical plaque and a tour
questions.explanation: 'I am asking for two things. First… Second…'
questions.hint: Look at Vinh's turn after An mentions a museum.

questions.prompt: Why does Vinh insist the new work be 'visibly new'?
options: Because new is more beautiful than old
Because a building should have visible layers, like a tree has rings — a visitor in 100 years should be able to see and distinguish 1934 from 2027
Because architects sign their work for fame
questions.explanation: 'A building should have visible layers, like a tree has rings.'
questions.hint: Look at Vinh's turn after An's 'honest renovation' phrase.

questions.prompt: What does Vinh mean by 'If you erase 2027 to look more 1934, you lie about both'?
options: Pretending the new work is original misrepresents both periods — the original is overstated, and the present is hidden
questions.explanation: The forgery distorts both eras at once.
questions.hint: Look at Vinh's image about layers and rings.

questions.prompt: An summarises Vinh's proposal in one phrase. What is it?
options: An honest renovation rather than a forged restoration
questions.explanation: 'An honest renovation rather than a forged restoration.'
questions.hint: Look at An's turn after Vinh's two-part proposal.

questions.prompt: What does An admit at the end of the conversation?
options: That he can defend half of Vinh's ideas to his clients; the other half costs more than they will pay
That he will demolish the building
questions.explanation: 'I can defend half of what you've said to my clients. The other half costs more than they will pay.'

questions.prompt: What does Vinh propose as a next step before An signs anything?
options: Walk the building one afternoon together — there may be rooms whose proportions can be kept at almost no extra cost
questions.explanation: 'Invite me to walk the building one afternoon. There may be rooms whose proportions can be kept at almost no extra cost…'
questions.hint: Look at Vinh's response to An's budget remark.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closer to Vinh's central position?
options: Old buildings should never be touched.
Old buildings should not be 'falsified'; they should be allowed visible layers, with new work clearly new and important original decisions preserved where possible.
The only honest renovation is total restoration.
questions.explanation: His two requests and the tree-rings image both express this.
questions.hint: Look at the two-part proposal and the tree-rings image.

questions.prompt: Which of these is Vinh NOT claiming?
options: That the building should be preserved as a museum, with no flats
That the front wall is the cheapest part of the building
That ratios and proportions are deliberate decisions worth keeping
That new work should be visibly new and dated
questions.explanation: 'I am not asking for a museum, An.' He explicitly rejects that framing.
questions.hint: Look at Vinh's turn after An says 'so your alternative is a museum'.

questions.prompt: What is the overall tone of the exchange?
options: Sharp but constructive — they push each other, neither concedes the whole, and they agree on a concrete next step (a Thursday walk-through)
questions.explanation: Both sides are firm; both make moves toward a middle. Vinh agrees there is 'a real negotiation' to be had; An agrees to Thursday.
questions.hint: Notice how each handles the other's strongest objection.

questions.prompt: What does Vinh promise to bring to the Thursday meeting?
options: His notes from 1981, when he was last inside the building
questions.explanation: 'I'll bring my notes from 1981, when I was last inside.'
questions.hint: Look at the very last turn.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Bác Vinh says a building should 'have visible layers, like a tree has rings'. Think of something in your life — a city you have lived in, a story your family tells, an old photograph that has been touched up — that has been smoothed into a single, false layer. What was kept, what was lost, and what would it mean to make the layers visible again?
